The Hidden Link Between Animal Agriculture and the Dutch Housing Crisis
Over half the Netherlands is devoted to raising animals and growing their feed. Seven percent is left for people to live in.

The Netherlands has a housing crisis. Waiting lists stretch for years. Construction projects stall under nitrogen regulations. Prices climb faster than wages. Everyone agrees the situation is dire, and everyone blames something different — immigration, zoning laws, NIMBYism, interest rates. Almost nobody talks about the cows. ## The numbers When you add up the land used for livestock farming and the crops grown to feed those animals, animal agriculture occupies half the country's land surface. Residential housing accounts for roughly seven percent. {/* TODO: embed interactive treemap visualisation */} | Category | Area (km²) | Share | |---|---|---| | **Animal farming** | **14,401** | **42.8%** | | Other farming | 5,523 | 16.4% | | Woodland and nature | 5,015 | 14.9% | | **Residential** | **2,414** | **7.2%** | | **Livestock feed** | **2,381** | **7.1%** | | Non-residential built-up | 1,680 | 5.0% | | Transport | 1,151 | 3.4% | | Recreation | 1,083 | 3.2% | That's a 7-to-1 ratio. For every square kilometre of housing, seven square kilometres are used to raise animals or grow their feed. The arable crop data makes the picture even clearer. Of the 651,805 hectares of arable cropland in the Netherlands, 41% grows animal feed — dominated by silage maize (green maize), which alone occupies 179,896 hectares. That single feed crop takes up more land than all Dutch potato cultivation (158,077 ha) or all sugar beet (80,634 ha). ### How we calculated this These figures combine two public datasets from CBS (Centraal Bureau voor de Statistiek), the Dutch government's statistics agency: 1. **Land use survey** ([Bodemgebruik, 2017](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/en/dataset/70262ENG/table)) — breaks the Netherlands into categories like agriculture, residential, transport, woodland, and water. This gives us the total agricultural area (2,230,445 ha) and all non-agricultural categories. 2. **Agricultural census** ([Landbouwtelling, 2023](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/en/dataset/80783ENG/table)) — breaks farm land down by type: grazing livestock, granivores, field crops, horticulture, and so on. This lets us split the agriculture total into animal farming, livestock feed, and other farming. 3. **Arable crops production** ([CBS 7100eng, 2023](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/en/dataset/7100eng/table)) — lists the area under cultivation for each arable crop. We classify each crop by its primary use (feed, food, or industrial) to calculate what share of arable cropland grows animal feed, replacing a previous rough estimate with a data-driven figure. A few methodological notes: - **Water surfaces are excluded.** The Netherlands' total area includes the IJsselmeer, the Wadden Sea, and the North Sea coast — roughly 7,900 km² of water. Including them would inflate the denominator and obscure how *land* is actually used. All percentages are calculated against land surface only (33,648 km²). - **The livestock feed share (41%) is calculated from CBS crop area data.** Each arable crop is classified by its primary use. Crops grown exclusively for feed (silage maize, corn cob mix, grain maize, triticale) are counted at 100%. Mixed-use cereals (wheat, barley, rye, oats) are assigned partial feed shares based on typical Dutch use patterns. - **The agricultural census and land use survey use different methodologies** and cover slightly different totals. We use the census to calculate *proportions* (what share of farm land goes to animal vs. crop farming), then apply those proportions to the land use survey's total. This avoids double-counting. The analysis script that generates these numbers from the raw CBS data is [available in the site's repository](https://github.com/stephanoumenos/website/blob/main/scripts/land-use-analysis.ts). You can run it yourself: `npm run land-use`. ## The nitrogen bottleneck The housing shortage isn't just a matter of land. It's also a matter of chemistry. The Netherlands has some of the strictest nitrogen emission regulations in Europe, introduced after courts ruled the government had failed to protect Natura 2000 habitats. These regulations have brought construction to a near standstill in many regions. The building industry accounts for roughly 0.6% of the country's nitrogen emissions. Animal agriculture accounts for over 50%. The arithmetic is hard to ignore: the sector responsible for the smallest fraction of nitrogen emissions is bearing the heaviest regulatory burden, while the sector responsible for the largest fraction continues to operate largely unimpeded. ## Following the money The imbalance extends beyond Dutch borders. According to a [2024 study from the University of Leiden](https://www.universiteitleiden.nl/en/news/2024/04/how-eu-farm-subsidies-favour-high-emission-animal-products), more than 80% of EU farm subsidies flow to animal farming and feed production — the same sector responsible for an estimated 84% of the EU's food-related greenhouse gas emissions. In other words, European taxpayers are financing the very industry that makes it harder to build homes in the Netherlands. ## A constitutional question The Dutch Constitution is unusually explicit about the government's obligations. Article 21 requires authorities to keep the country habitable and protect the environment. Article 22 mandates the promotion of public health and the provision of sufficient housing. By any honest reading, a policy regime that allocates over half the nation's land to a single industry — one that simultaneously dominates nitrogen emissions, contributes to climate change, and crowds out residential construction — sits uncomfortably with those obligations. ## The broader picture The housing crisis is one consequence among many. Animal agriculture is the leading driver of deforestation and biodiversity loss worldwide. It is a significant contributor to antibiotic resistance. It has been linked to the emergence of zoonotic diseases — including, in recent years, avian influenza outbreaks that have swept through Dutch farms. The health effects of diets high in processed animal products are well documented in epidemiological literature. And then there is the matter that tends to make people uncomfortable: the roughly 100 billion land animals killed globally each year, most of them in conditions that no one who has seen them would willingly defend. These are not separate issues. They share a root cause. ## What comes next None of this requires a radical imagination. It requires looking at data the government already collects and asking straightforward questions about how we use our land. A few starting points: - **Reconsider your own choices.** Demand shapes supply. Every meal is a decision. - **Share the data.** Most people have never seen these numbers presented together. That alone is worth something. - **Demand policy coherence.** If nitrogen regulations can halt housing construction, they should apply proportionally to the sector that produces most of the nitrogen. The Netherlands is a small, densely populated country with a large agricultural sector and a severe housing shortage. The connection between the two is not hidden. It's just not talked about. --- *Land use data: [CBS Bodemgebruik 2017](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/en/dataset/70262ENG/table). Farm type breakdown: [CBS Landbouwtelling 2023](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/en/dataset/80783ENG/table). Arable crops: [CBS 7100eng, 2023](https://opendata.cbs.nl/#/CBS/en/dataset/7100eng/table). Nitrogen figures: RIVM. Analysis script: [`scripts/land-use-analysis.ts`](https://github.com/stephanoumenos/website/blob/main/scripts/land-use-analysis.ts).*
Go Interfaces Are Not What You Think
How implicit interface satisfaction changed the way I design software.

When I first learned Go, interfaces felt limiting. No generics (at the time), no inheritance,
no abstract classes. Just interfaces with a handful of methods.

Then I understood implicit satisfaction, and everything clicked.

## Implicit, not explicit

In Java or C#, you declare that a type implements an interface:

```java
class MyWriter implements Writer { ... }
```

In Go, you don't. A type satisfies an interface simply by having the right methods:

```go
type Writer interface {
    Write(p []byte) (n int, err error)
}

// MyWriter satisfies Writer — no declaration needed.
type MyWriter struct{}

func (w MyWriter) Write(p []byte) (n int, err error) {
    // ...
    return len(p), nil
}
```

This one decision has large consequences for how you design APIs.

## What it unlocks

Because Go interfaces are implicit, you can define an interface *at the point of use* rather than
at the point of implementation. This is the crucial thing.

Say you're writing a function that needs to send a notification. Instead of importing some
`Notifier` interface defined by a dependency, you define exactly what you need:

```go
type Sender interface {
    Send(ctx context.Context, msg string) error
}

func notify(s Sender, msg string) error {
    return s.Send(context.Background(), msg)
}
```

Any type with a `Send` method works — whether it's your email client, your Slack webhook, or a
test fake. The function owns its dependency contract.

## The design principle

This leads to a principle I now follow consistently: **keep interfaces small and define them
where they're used, not where they're implemented.**

The standard library models this perfectly. `io.Reader` has one method. `io.Writer` has one method.
`io.Closer` has one method. They compose:

```go
type ReadWriteCloser interface {
    Reader
    Writer
    Closer
}
```

When you define a function that takes an `io.Reader`, it works with files, network connections,
bytes buffers, test readers — anything. The interface is so minimal that almost anything satisfies it.

## Testing becomes effortless

The biggest practical benefit: testing. When your functions depend on small interfaces rather than
concrete types, you can write test fakes in three lines:

```go
type fakeSender struct{ sent []string }

func (f *fakeSender) Send(_ context.Context, msg string) error {
    f.sent = append(f.sent, msg)
    return nil
}
```

No mocking library needed. No reflection. Just a struct that satisfies the interface your function
needs.

---

Go's interfaces taught me to think about dependencies differently. The question isn't "what does
this type provide?" but "what does this function need?" It's a subtle shift, but once you feel it,
you can't go back.
The ethical argument is simple. Sticking to it requires something else entirely.

I became vegan for the animals. I stayed vegan because it became who I am.

The argument is simple enough: if it's wrong to cause unnecessary suffering, and we can live well
without eating animals, then we should. Once you take that seriously, it's hard to argue with.

But logic alone doesn't sustain a lifestyle. What sustains it is finding people who live it,
cooking food you genuinely love, and letting it become ordinary.

## The first month

The first month was hard — not because of cravings, exactly, but because of social friction.
Every meal with friends became a negotiation. Every restaurant menu became a puzzle.
I felt conspicuous in a way I hadn't anticipated.

What helped was reframing the question. Instead of asking *what can I not eat*, I started asking
*what do I actually want to eat*. That shift made all the difference.

## What nobody tells you

Nobody tells you that going vegan will make you a better cook. When you remove the default
options — the quick chicken, the easy burger — you're forced to actually think about food.
You start exploring cuisines that were always mostly plant-based: Indian, Ethiopian, Thai, Lebanese.

You start paying attention in a way you never did before.

## On purity

One thing I've learned: veganism doesn't have to be about purity. It's about reducing harm where
you reasonably can. The perfect shouldn't be the enemy of the good.

If someone eats plant-based 80% of the time, that matters. If they slip up, that's fine. The goal
isn't a clean record — it's a lighter footprint.

I say this as someone who wasted years being self-righteous about it. Don't be that person.

---

If you're curious and want to try it, start with one meal a day. You don't need to announce it
to anyone. Just try it.
